step_by_step_thinking: I agree with the consensus on this matter. A key aspect of Eisenmenger syndrome is its transition into right-to-left shunt status, where typical auscultatory findings shift away from those characteristic of early stages—such as a single S2—which might persist if there remains significant left-to-right shunting despite increasing pulmonary hypertension. Therefore, the absence of splitting or even presentation of a single S2 can be interpreted as a marker moving closer to irreversible damage associated with advanced Eisenmenger disease. This supports the exclusion of option B correctly.
raw_response:
I agree with the consensus on this matter. A key aspect of Eisenmenger syndrome is its transition into right-to-left shunt status, where typical auscultatory findings shift away from those characteristic of early stages—such as a single S2—which might persist if there remains significant left-to-right shunting despite increasing pulmonary hypertension. Therefore, the absence of splitting or even presentation of a single S2 can be interpreted as a marker moving closer to irreversible damage associated with advanced Eisenmenger disease. This supports the exclusion of option B correctly. 

Answer: B. Disagreeing would imply overlooking crucial diagnostic clues specific to late-stage Eisenmenger syndrome development. Thus, my final stance adheres firmly to the provided rationale. Answer Choice: B.
